cover.html:
[B] Welcome in, everyone. Today we're talking about the worst part of running Kubernetes — the 3 AM part — and how it's about to look completely different. I've got Jesse Miller with me, who owns a lot of this on the product side.
[J] Glad to be here. Fair warning: about halfway through we stop talking and I make Bahubali break a real cluster on camera.
[B] That's the plan. Let's go.
safe-harbor.html: [B] Quick legal beat — some of what we show is forward-looking, so make your buying decisions on what's generally available. Ten seconds, and we move on.
intro-presenters.html:
[B] Thirty seconds on us. I spend my days in customers' clusters helping SRE teams stop fires. Jesse — you decide what we build to make that possible.
[J] Right. My job is basically to make sure that when you're staring at a CrashLoopBackOff, the product already did the boring 40 minutes of correlation for you. We'll come back to that phrase — the boring 40 minutes — a lot today.
[B] So the format: I'm going to ask Jesse the questions I actually get asked by SREs. He answers. Then we go prove it live.
hook-3am.html:
[B] Here's the moment we're designing for. It's 3 AM. A pod is in CrashLoopBackOff. What does that hour actually look like today, Jesse?
[J] It looks like tab roulette. You've got restart counts in one tool, memory limits in a second, the log line that matters in a third, and the k8s events in a fourth — and none of them share an identity for the pod. So the human becomes the join key. At 3 AM. That's the problem. It's not that the data doesn't exist — it's that nothing correlated it for you.
[B] And every one of those tabs is a place to lose ten minutes.
[J] Exactly. Our whole thesis is: the machine should do the correlation, and the human should get an answer. Everything after this slide is us earning that claim.
metrics-myth.html:
[B] I have to start here, because it's the number-one objection I hear: 'Isn't Elastic just logs? For metrics I'll keep Prometheus or Datadog.' Jesse — kill that myth.
[J] Happily. Elastic has a real metrics database underneath — a columnar time-series engine we built specifically for metrics. Not logs with a metrics label on top. Purpose-built columnar storage, a metrics query path, and — this is the part people don't expect — it's engineered for high cardinality. Kubernetes is the ultimate cardinality monster: every pod, container, namespace, node is a new series. That's exactly the workload this engine eats.
[B] And the reason people churn off other tools at scale is the bill.
[J] That's the whole point. High cardinality is where the other vendors' pricing explodes. We re-engineered storage so you can keep all the data — which, spoiler, is what makes the AI part actually work — without the cardinality tax. So the answer to the objection is: metrics, at Kubernetes scale, on a budget you can defend to your CFO.
[B] Let's show them the engine.
columnar-metrics-tech.html:
[B] This is the engine. Three things, Jesse — the headline of each pillar.
[J] Left: query performance — vectorized, parallel column reads, so investigations come back fast, which matters a lot once AI is doing the querying for you. Middle: storage efficiency — true columnar files, doc-value skippers instead of heavyweight indices. Last year we stored a datapoint in ~25 bytes; now it's under 4. That ~6x is why keeping all the data is affordable. Right: it speaks Prometheus and PromQL natively — same protocol in, same query language, one storage layout underneath.
[B] So for the SRE that already lives in Prometheus — what changes for them?
[J] Nothing about their instrumentation. Same wire protocol, same queries — better engine, cheaper storage. We'll hit that on the next slide.
[B] The thing I want people to hold onto: store more, cheaper isn't a nice-to-have. It's the fuel for the AI investigation you're about to see.
competitive.html:
[B] Put numbers on it. When I'm in a competitive deal, what do I say?
[J] Faster queries than Prometheus and Mimir, strong edge on ClickHouse, storage efficiency that beats the field, and PromQL compatibility so there's no relearning. And on Datadog specifically — meaningfully cheaper at the same cardinality. The pitch isn't 'switch and compromise.' It's 'same language, upgrade the engine, cut the bill.'
[B] That's the framing I use: it's an upgrade, not a migration.
prometheus.html:
[B] So concretely — an SRE running Prometheus and Grafana today. What's the switching cost?
[J] Close to zero to try. Point your Prometheus remote-write at Elastic, keep writing PromQL, keep your Grafana muscle memory. You can be live in a day, and the dashboards and alerts convert over. The whole design principle is: don't ask the SRE to relearn their job. Give them their world, faster and cheaper — and then layer the new stuff on top.
[B] Which is the perfect setup for the actually-new stuff. Let's talk about the easy button.
[Backup] promql.html is disabled — pull it in live if someone asks 'does Kibana really speak PromQL.'
kubernetes.html:
[B] Jesse — I don't want to build observability. I want to turn it on. What do I get out of the box?
[J] The new Kubernetes integration ships with the dashboards already built — and built for drill-down, not just pretty tiles. It ships alert templates that fire on day one: CrashLoopBackOff, OOMKilled, pending pods, node disk pressure — the classics, pre-wired. And ML anomaly detection that learns a baseline per workload, so it flags a memory-growth trajectory before it becomes an incident.
[B] Per-workload is the key word — my payments service and my batch job don't behave the same.
[J] Right, a global threshold is useless. The ML learns 'normal' for each workload. And — Bahubali, look at the cluster list on screen.
[B] Wait, that's your robot-shop cluster, Jesse. Are we about to break your cluster on this webinar?
[J] We absolutely are. That's the demo. But first — quick detour. That bullet list said 'Agent Skills' and 'Kubernetes Workflows.' Those aren't two names for the same thing, and picking the wrong one is a real mistake teams make. Thirty seconds on the difference, then we go break something.
alert-to-answer.html:
[B] This is the piece I think people underestimate. Walk me through what happens the instant an alert fires.
[J] The Investigation Workflow runs the diagnosis automatically — before you even open the alert. It checks the termination reason from the k8s API. It consults the ML anomaly index to see what was already abnormal. It assesses upstream service health against a 7-day baseline to catch a cascade. And it writes a structured root-cause hypothesis — with the evidence it used and recommended next steps.
[B] So when the human wakes up and clicks the page, what do they actually see?
[J] Not a link to six dashboards. They get an answer to react to. The boring 40 minutes already happened. That's the shift — the human moves from investigator to decision-maker.
[B] And for teams that live in an IDE or an agent, this same depth follows them. Which is the next slide.
skills-vs-workflows.html:
[B] Wait — before we break anything: 'Agent Skills' and 'Kubernetes Workflows' were both on that last slide. Aren't those the same idea with two names?
[J] No, and mixing them up is the mistake I see teams make. Tool: a single lookup or action — no judgment involved, so a script can do it. Skill: instructions we hand an agent — knowledge is the value, and it's the right call when a person's driving and you can afford a frontier-tier model. Workflow: the steps run as fixed code, regardless of what the model 'thinks.'
[B] That sounds like a preference, not a rule.
[J] It would be, if we hadn't measured it. We ran the same real incidents through all three, on a strong model and a cheap one. On the expensive model, a good skill can basically tie the workflow — the knowledge really is the value there. Drop to a budget model and the skill's accuracy falls off a cliff, 10 to 60 percent, while the workflow barely moves, still around 90. It's also roughly 10x more consistent run-to-run — even on the expensive model. And the one thing a skill structurally can't do: run itself. A workflow fires the instant the alert does, zero agents required.
[B] So what's the rule of thumb?
[J] Unattended or alert-triggered, needs to hold up on a cheap model, or needs a rock-solid floor? Workflow. Interactive, human's driving, knowledge is the differentiator? Skill. Single action, no judgment call? Tool. This diagram is the actual shape of the CrashLoopBackOff workflow we ship — characterize what happened, branch on whether it's memory-related, corroborate against the environment, synthesize. Same idea as the alert-to-answer flow a minute ago — this is what's actually running underneath it.
[B] Let's go see it for real.
workflows.html:
[B] So here's the real thing, not just the diagram.
[J] This is the Workflows page in Kibana — the exact CrashLoopBackOff investigation we just walked through, as a real declarative definition. It correlates metrics, logs, traces, and anomalies, and it kicks off on any of three triggers: an alert firing, a schedule, or on failure of something else. Notice it can call out to an Agent Builder agent for the one or two steps that genuinely need a model's judgment — classifying a log pattern, say — and it writes the finding straight into a Case, so it's a tracked artifact, not just a chat answer.
[B] And that's where Agent Skills show up too.
[J] Right — they compose. A skill can extend what a workflow synthesizes, or point back to the workflow when a step needs the guaranteed version. We'll drive this exact workflow live in a few minutes.
discover-metrics.html:
[B] Zoom out for a second — what if I don't have an alert yet, I just have a hunch about a metric?
[J] That's Discover. One ES|QL query using the TS command — no dashboard, no visualization builder — and it auto-charts whatever series come back. On this cluster that's 427 metrics charted instantly from a single query. Filter with WHERE, break down by any dimension, and from each chart you can inspect it, drop it on a dashboard, or add it straight to a case.
[B] So the workflow handles the alert you already know about.
[J] And Discover handles the metric you didn't have a name for yet. Same engine underneath both.
mcp-demo.html:
[B] Not everyone lives in Kibana. My SREs live in Claude, in Cursor, in the terminal. What do they get?
[J] The Elastic Observability MCP app exposes the same investigation depth to any MCP-compatible client — Claude, Cursor, whatever. Cluster health, service dependency graphs, blast-radius analysis, even alert creation — all inline, without leaving the IDE. So the on-call engineer can ask, in plain English, 'what's the blast radius of the cart service right now,' and get a real answer grounded in the metrics engine.
[B] Which means the second half of our demo is: do the exact same investigation we just did in the UI — but from inside Claude.
[J] Same data, same brains, different surface. Let's go break something.
[Backup] plain-english.html is disabled — pull it in if you want to dwell on the conversational angle.
demo-mechanism.html:
[B] Before we go live — quick heads-up on exactly what we're about to do, so nobody walks away thinking a random OOM cascaded through the cluster.
[J] It's more targeted than that. The `recommendation` service has a cache with a leak flag — we flip it on, and its memory starts growing unbounded. On its own that's slow, so we also tighten its memory limit just below the current footprint, which forces it to cross the ceiling in minutes instead of hours. Once it does: OOMKilled, Kubernetes restarts it, it fills right back up — about a 90-second cycle. Five-plus restarts inside roughly ten minutes trips CrashLoopBackOff.
[B] And what should we expect to see light up, in order?
[J] If the ML job's baselined, it flags the memory creep before anything actually breaks — the hard signal, since a threshold can't see a slow leak coming. Then the CrashLoopBackOff alert fires alongside an Availability SLO burning its error budget fast — that's an availability failure, not an error-rate one. Then the blast radius: frontend and frontend-proxy's error rate climbs as their calls to recommendation start failing. And the actual smoking gun is in the k8s events — BackOff, Killing — not the app logs, which just show normal startup lines.
[B] So to be really clear — is this a cluster-wide event?
[J] No. One service, one injected leak. The failure fans out through its callers' error rate — it doesn't cascade through cluster memory. That's exactly the distinction the workflow's synthesis step has to get right: anchor on recommendation's own OOM, and reject the blast-radius services as the cause.
[B] Let's go do it.
demo-transition.html:
[B] Alright — slides down, real environment up. Here's what we're going to do, and I'll narrate the whole way. Jesse, set the scene.
[J] We've got the robot-shop app running on a cluster. You just saw exactly how we're about to break it — flip the leak flag, tighten the limit, watch it crash-loop. Then we watch Elastic catch it, correlate it, and hand us a root cause — twice. Once in the UI, once in Claude.

=== LIVE DEMO — Part A: Elastic UI (~8–9 min) ===
1. Steady state (30s): OOTB K8s dashboard, jmiller-robot-shop green. 'This is normal. Remember this shape.'
2. Break it (1 min): flip the leak flag, apply the tightened memory limit; restarts climb; OOMKilled appears as termination reason.
3. Alert fires (1 min): day-one CrashLoopBackOff template triggers. 'Nobody configured this. It shipped.'
4. ML already knew (1–2 min): open anomaly detection — memory-growth flagged before the crash. 'Baseline is per-workload. No threshold needed.'
5. Investigation ran itself (2–3 min): open alert → auto-generated root-cause hypothesis (termination reason, correlated ML anomaly, upstream health vs 7-day baseline, blast radius, next steps). 'I opened this AFTER it was diagnosed.'
6. Drill-down proof (1 min): click one piece of evidence — real, queryable data from the metrics engine.
PRESENTER CUE: if ingest lag makes the break slow, say 'while that propagates...' and narrate the ML story on running data. Have a pre-broken workload ready as fallback.

=== LIVE DEMO — Part B: Claude + MCP (~6–7 min) ===
1. Switch to Claude (30s): 'Same incident. Now I'm an SRE who lives in my IDE. I never open Kibana.'
2. Ask in plain English (2 min): 'What's unhealthy in jmiller-robot-shop right now, and why?' → MCP returns cluster health + the OOMKilled workload, grounded in real metrics.
3. Blast radius (2 min): 'What's the blast radius of that workload?' → dependency graph / upstream impact inline.
4. Agentic RCA (1–2 min): Claude walks the root cause via the Elastic skills — same hypothesis, reasoned conversationally.
5. Optional act (1 min): create an alert / draft remediation from inside Claude.
KEY LINE TO LAND: 'Two surfaces, one metrics engine, one investigation. The human never became the join key.'
common-cases.html:
[B] Coming out of that — I don't want anyone thinking 'cool demo, but my incidents are boring.' These ARE the boring ones. Jesse, this grid is basically every ticket my SREs write.
[J] That's the point. CrashLoopBackOff, OOMKilled, pending pods, disk pressure, a slow memory leak, a cascade — every one of these has a day-one alert and a signal path already wired. You're not building detection for the common cases. They ship on. The ML and the investigation are what turn the nasty version of each of these into a two-minute read instead of a two-hour night.
[B] The easy button — for the stuff that actually pages you.
thank-you.html:
[B] So — what do people do Monday?
[J] Three doors. Already on Elastic? Turn on the Kubernetes integration and you've got the dashboards, the alerts, and the ML today. On Prometheus or Grafana? Point remote-write at us — you're live in a day and nothing about your queries changes. On Datadog and feeling the cardinality bill? Same data, lower cost, and this whole investigation experience on top.
[B] And if you're in an agentic workflow — grab the Observability MCP app and put this inside Claude, like we just did.
[J] One engine. Metrics at Kubernetes scale, on budget. An easy button for the common cases, and an AI that does the boring 40 minutes so your on-call gets an answer instead of a page.
[B] Thanks everyone — we'll take questions now, and we're both around after. Let's get into it.
